plain technology catalog
  • C# — My main direction for .NET desktop work and the language I want to push further into game development.
  • Java — University projects, desktop interfaces, algorithms, and the first tower-defense learning project.
  • Python — Backend prototypes, automation, APIs, data handling, and the occasional script that becomes a whole project.
  • JavaScript — Interactive web work, visualization, browser experiments, and product interfaces.
  • C — Low-level programming, manual memory work, parsing, and graphics fundamentals.
  • Git — Version control for every project that survives beyond the first experiment.
  • SQL — Relational data modeling, queries, and making persistent state less mysterious.
  • .NET — The runtime and tooling behind my current C# desktop work.
  • FastAPI — A practical Python framework for structured API prototypes.
  • Next.js — Product-oriented web applications and interface prototypes; not used to render this static site.
  • React — Component-based interfaces for application work when a static page is not enough.
  • Supabase — Hosted relational data and application services for product prototypes.
  • Three.js — Browser-based 3D scenes and geographic visualization.
  • SDL3 — A small graphics and input layer for low-level C experiments.
  • Altium Designer — PCB layout work for an autonomous-vehicle team project.
  • Unity — The main engine currently being explored for C# game-development workflows. This is a learning path, not professional production experience.
